person.name: Shiney Rashmitha Chandraganti
person.title: Cancer Immunology Researcher
alumniOf: Motilal Nehru National Institute of Technology Allahabad

hero.headline: I study how tumor state shapes immune response to immunotherapy.
hero.lead: I am a cancer immunology researcher in the Department of Biomedical Sciences at Cornell University. My current work focuses on CD4+ T-cell function, tumor plasticity, and response to immune checkpoint blockade in breast cancer.
hero.support: Across in vivo tumor models, CRISPR/Cas9 perturbations, multicolor flow cytometry, and immunofluorescence, I study why some tumors remain resistant and what makes them become newly responsive.

items.label: Current focus
items.value: Breast cancer immunology and checkpoint response

items.label: modes of inquiry: bench, clinical, and computational

about.title: I came to cancer immunology through a longer interest in how biology changes with context.
paragraphs: My research path has moved across microbial systems, biomaterials, computational screening, public health, and tumor immunology. What connects those experiences is a consistent interest in how biological systems respond to environmental and molecular change, and how that knowledge can be used to improve treatment.

I am especially interested in the tumor microenvironment, immune-cell behavior, and resistance to therapy. Long term, I hope to contribute to better immunotherapies and to mentor future researchers working across experimental and translational biology.

sideCards.title: How I approach research
items: I prefer mechanistic questions over descriptive ones whenever possible.
I trust conclusions more when they are supported by orthogonal methods.
I value careful experimental design, troubleshooting, and reproducibility.
I see mentorship, lab systems, and scientific rigor as part of the same work.

contact.title: I am open to doctoral opportunities, research collaborations, and conversations in cancer immunology.
contact.body: If you would like to connect about my work, publications, or future research, I would be glad to hear from you.
